ScopeThe methodThe five stepsThe full essay (Bruce Nielsen)What we cannot account forWhere to look
/sources/

Sources & databases

The record only holds because it points outward. These are the sources Bruce Nielsen reasons against — the exact resources cited in his essay, linked. Filter by the question they help answer.

Yad Vashem — Central Database of Shoah Victims’ Names ↗victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
international
USHMM — Holocaust Survivors and Victims Database ↗victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
international
Claims Conference / Bundesarchiv — Gedenkbuch (Memorial Book) ↗victims
Answers: Who was the owner, and their fate?
Germany
JewishGen — Holocaust Database ↗victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
international
Looted Cultural Assets ↗victims
Answers: Whose bookplate is this?
Germany
Tentative List of Jewish Cultural Treasures in Axis-Occupied Countries ↗victims
Answers: Whose collection was it?
Europe
German Lost Art Foundation ↗victims
Answers: Whose collection was it?
Germany
Stolpersteine Project ↗victims
Answers: Who lived here, and their fate?
Europe
Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe — Room of Names ↗victims
Answers: Who was this person?
Europe
Joods Monument — Dutch victims of the Nazis ↗victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
Netherlands
KehilaLinks (JewishGen) — community pages ↗victims
Answers: Which community, and who lived there?
Europe
JDCRP — Documentation of Persecuted Jewish Collectors ↗victims
Answers: Whose private collection was it?
Europe
ERR Project — reconstruction of looted libraries ↗victims
Answers: Which library did the ERR take it from?
Western Europe
OAD Monthly Reports (fold3) ↗shipping
Answers: Was it sent here, and when?
Offenbach
“Institutional Library Collections – Germany,” 67 libraries (31 Mar 1946) ↗shipping
Answers: Which institution held it?
Germany
35 institutional Jewish libraries in Germany, total 33,499 (31 Jul 1946) ↗shipping
Answers: Which institution held it?
Germany
OAD list of 582 Jewish libraries, total 58,667 (30 Sep 1946) ↗shipping
Answers: Which institution held it?
Germany
“List of the Privately-owned (identifiable) Book Collections,” Wiesbaden (Oct 1949) ↗shipping
Answers: Whose private library was it?
Germany
RSHA — Reichssicherheitshauptamt, Bibliothek ↗shipping
Answers: Which library did the RSHA take it from?
Germany
Bencowitz — Library Markings, vol. I (Eastern), NLI ↗reference
Answers: What stamp is this?
Offenbach
Bencowitz — Library Markings, vol. II (Western), NLI ↗reference
Answers: What stamp is this?
Offenbach
Michael Krupp, “Schicksal von Büchern” (2014) ↗reference
Answers: What stamp is this?
Germany
PCHA — Plunder and Restitution (2000) ↗reference
Answers: How did this material reach the U.S.?
U.S.
Freidus Classification Shelf List (Dropsie)
bibliographic
Answers: Was it sourced as a JCR gift?
Philadelphia · not online
Rare-manuscripts accession list (Dropsie)
bibliographic
Answers: Did the manuscript come from JCR?
Philadelphia · not online
Josef Rosin, Protecting Our Litvak Heritage (2009) ↗baltic
Answers: Which Lithuanian community?
Lithuania
JewishGen — Yizkor Book Project ↗baltic
Answers: Which community, and who lived there?
Eastern Europe
Database of victims in Czechoslovakia ↗baltic
Answers: Who owned this book?
Czechoslovakia
JRI-Poland — Jewish records ↗baltic
Answers: Who owned this book?
Poland
JDC — Prague Emigration Case File Index (1945–50) ↗baltic
Answers: Where did the owner go?
Czechoslovakia
Provenance research & scholarship by Bruce Nielsen, Ph.D. — Judaica Public Services Librarian & Archivist, Penn Libraries (Katz Center for Advanced Judaic Studies)A site built by Judaica DH at Penn Libraries
